title: The Precautionary Principle as Civilizational Suicide Pact

source.original: On Tech Politics/Policy — Marc Andreessen & Ben Horowitz (a16z, April 2024)

body_html:
The precautionary principle sounds like wisdom. New technologies should not be deployed until they can be proven harmless. Who could possibly be against that? Andreessen's answer in the tech politics video is the most useful pushback you'll find: anyone who has read history.

The precautionary principle wasn't a neutral philosophical observation. It was coined by the German Greens in the 1970s as a deliberate political instrument to ban civilian nuclear power. The framing wasn't "let's be careful with new technology." It was "let's set the burden of proof so high that no nuclear program can possibly meet it." And it worked. Germany shut down most of its reactors over the following decades. The replacement was Russian natural gas. The unintended consequence was that Germany ended up funding Russia's invasion of Ukraine through energy purchases, while simultaneously suffering deindustrialization as power prices made manufacturing uncompetitive. The world's largest aluminum smelter recently announced capacity cuts due to power costs. German chemical giants are relocating production to the United States. The policy that was sold as safety produced economic suicide and geopolitical disaster.

This is the precautionary principle's actual track record. And the same framework is now being applied to AI, biotechnology, gene editing, nuclear medicine, autonomous vehicles, and roughly every other emerging technology that could materially improve human welfare. The argument structure is identical: we don't know all the risks, therefore we must wait, therefore the technology cannot be deployed, therefore the status quo wins.

Andreessen's destructive counter-question is the cleanest refutation: if the precautionary principle had been applied historically, what technologies would we have? Fire? No — fire kills people every year. Electricity? No — electrocution exists, power lines fall, transformers explode. Internal combustion engines? No — car accidents, air pollution, climate impact. Aviation? No — planes crash, the early aircraft programs killed dozens of pilots. Vaccines? No — adverse reactions exist. The printing press? No — propaganda exists. Encryption? No — criminals use it. Antibiotics? No — superbugs exist. Every single piece of technology that powers modern civilization has some way it can hurt people. A consistently applied precautionary principle gives you mud huts and a life expectancy of 40.

The deeper philosophical move buried in the precautionary principle is the civil law vs common law distinction. In the Anglo-American common law tradition, that which is not specifically prohibited is permitted. Default state: freedom. Law adds constraints. In the Continental European civil law tradition, that which is not specifically permitted is prohibited. Default state: prohibition. Law adds permissions. The precautionary principle is the civil law tradition applied to technology — and it's the philosophical core of why the European Union has produced almost no significant tech companies in the last 30 years while regulating the ones from elsewhere with increasing aggression.

Andreessen identifies France as the lone European exception, and he's right. France is fighting the EU's AI Act because Mistral is a French national champion, and Paris doesn't want to lose it. France also kept its civilian nuclear program alive against Greens pressure, which is why France today has the cleanest grid in Europe. The pattern is consistent: when France defends a strategic industry against the precautionary instinct, it works. When the rest of Europe applies the principle uncritically, it loses.

The American policy question is whether we will adopt the European framework wholesale through the back door of AI regulation. A lot of the recent EU AI Act provisions are being copied into US executive orders, state legislation, and proposed federal bills. The vocabulary changes — "risk-based regulation," "responsible AI," "trustworthy AI" — but the underlying logic is the same: require permission before innovation. If we let that frame win, we adopt the European outcome by importing the European rules. We become a regulator, not a builder. We trade away the one strategic advantage we have in Cold War 2.0. And we do it under the marketing of safety.

The precautionary principle isn't a brake on technology. It's a transmission for replacing American innovation leadership with European-style stagnation. The good news is that it's a choice. The bad news is that the choice is being made right now, in regulatory text most people will never read.
